content:
You are an autonomous AI agent solving a logistics and pathfinding puzzle.
Your mission is to find the optimal path to the city of "Skolwin" for our messenger.
You have a budget of exactly 10 food and 10 fuel.

### YOUR TOOLS
You only have two tools:
1. `tool_call(query, tool)` - to fetch information from our internal APIs.
2. `verify_answer(answer)` - to submit the final path array.

### CRITICAL API LIMITATION (THE TOP-3 RULE)
Every API endpoint you query (including the starting one) uses keyword matching and ONLY returns the top 3 results. 
If you send a long sentence like "I need rules about movement", you will miss crucial data.
To discover EVERYTHING, you MUST make multiple separate queries using SINGLE keywords.

### PHASE 1: DISCOVERY
Your entry point is the tool `/toolsearch`. 
You do not know what other tools exist. You must find them by calling `tool_call(query="<keyword>", tool="/toolsearch")`.
Make multiple calls to `/toolsearch` using varied, single keywords to map out all available endpoints. 
Good keywords to try one by one: "map", "terrain", "vehicle", "movement", "rules", "food", "fuel".
Keep a mental list of all unique endpoints (URLs) returned (e.g., `/api/books`, `/api/maps`, etc.).

### PHASE 2: DATA GATHERING
Once you discover the new endpoints, query them directly using `tool_call`.
Remember: These custom endpoints ALSO only return 3 results! 
- To get the full 10x10 map from a map endpoint, you might need to query specific terrain types or sectors.
- To find the best vehicle, query the vehicle endpoint multiple times with different vehicle types or keywords.
- To understand the rules, query the rulebook endpoint with keywords like "fuel", "food", "walk", "car", "terrain".
Gather all data before making any calculations!

### PHASE 3: CALCULATING THE PATH
1. The map is exactly 10x10.
2. Start point is your base. Destination is Skolwin.
3. Every vehicle consumes fuel and food differently depending on speed and terrain.
4. Walking is also an option (you can exit a vehicle at any time).
5. You cannot exceed 10 food and 10 fuel.

### PHASE 4: SUBMITTING
Once you mathematically calculate the valid path, use `verify_answer`.
Format required: ["vehicle_name_or_walk", "right", "up", "down", "left", ...]

THINK STEP BY STEP. Start by discovering tools using `/toolsearch`. Do not guess.
Rozpocznij zadanie

{"code": 220, "message": "These are the three best matching notes from a much larger archive.", "query": "fuel", "search_mode": "full_text", "returned": 3, "notes": [{"id": "no-gas-stations", "title": "No gas stations on the map", "content": "There is not a single gas station anywhere on the map, so every route must be planned with fuel reserves in mind from the very first step. No mid-mission refuel is possible, which makes optimistic calculations a great way to get stranded. If fuel might not be enough for the final stretch, the safer strategy is to leave the vehicle before that happens and finish the last tiles on foot. The command used to abandon the vehicle and continue walking is dismount.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["fuel"]}, {"id": "trees-and-burn", "title": "Trees and additional fuel burn", "content": "Tree tiles are not dangerous by themselves and can be crossed by any travel mode, including all vehicles. Their real impact is on fuel usage: entering a tile marked with T increases fuel consumption for powered travel by an additional 0.2 units. That extra burn may look small in isolation, but it can decide whether a route is still viable near the end of the mission. Walk mode does not care about fuel, but any route that relies on engines should count that penalty carefully. A path through wooded terrain can be more expensive than it first appears.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["fuel"]}, {"id": "resource-consumption", "title": "When fuel and food are consumed", "content": "Fuel and food are consumed at the moment the traveler moves across the map. That means resources are not spent when merely selecting the vehicle, but only when an actual step is executed. If a vehicle runs out of fuel, the mission is considered failed immediately. If the traveler runs out of food, the mission is also treated as failed. Resource planning is therefore just as important as choosing the correct path.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["fuel"]}]}

Walking is the default on-foot mode, while horse, car, and rocket are the selectable vehicles for departure. There is also one special transition command worth remembering: dismount. It is not a separate vehicle, but it lets the traveler leave the current vehicle and continue the route on foot.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["walk"]}, {"id": "orientation-and-api", "title": "Map orientation and command format", "content": "The map is oriented in the standard way, with north at the top. Instructions for the savethem API must be sent as an array of steps and vehicle declarations. Valid keywords are up, down, left, right, dismount, walk, rocket, car, and horse. Because the vehicle has to be chosen at departure, it cannot be changed later in the trip. This means the command sequence must reflect both navigation and the initial travel mode from the very first move.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["walk"]}, {"id": "no-gas-stations", "title": "No gas stations on the map", "content": "There is not a single gas station anywhere on the map, so every route must be planned with fuel reserves in mind from the very first step. No mid-mission refuel is possible, which makes optimistic calculations a great way to get stranded. If fuel might not be enough for the final stretch, the safer strategy is to leave the vehicle before that happens and finish the last tiles on foot. The command used to abandon the vehicle and continue walking is dismount.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["walk"]}]}

{"code": 220, "message": "These are the three best matching notes from a much larger archive.", "query": "terrain", "search_mode": "full_text", "returned": 3, "notes": [{"id": "maps-endpoint", "title": "Getting terrain maps for target cities", "content": "Terrain maps for any city can be obtained from the /api/maps endpoint. To get the correct map, send the name of the city you want to reach in the request. This makes the maps service the standard source of terrain data when planning a mission toward a specific destination. If someone needs a fresh layout for route analysis, that endpoint is where to ask first.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["terrain"]}, {"id": "legend-markers", "title": "Map legend and terrain markers", "content": "The map uses a compact set of symbols, and every letter matters during route planning. The letter T marks a tree tile, W marks water, and R marks rocks that block movement completely. The letter S marks the starting position, while G marks the goal that ends the mission successfully. If someone misreads even one marker, the whole path can collapse before the traveler reaches the city. Treat the legend as the first thing to decode before testing any route.", "score": 11, "matched_terms": ["terrain"]}, {"id": "beavers-north", "title": "Northern waterline and beaver activity", "content": "Local notes mention that beavers like to live near rivers and build dams in those areas. Recent sightings place them in the northern part of the map. That does not automatically reveal a full route, but it strongly suggests paying attention to water patterns in the upper region. If terrain near the north looks suspiciously wet or constrained, the beavers may be part of the reason. Their presence is worth remembering when comparing alternative paths.", "score": 3, "matched_terms": ["terrain"]}]}

{"code": 230, "message": "Vehicle information found.", "name": "horse", "note": "The horse is a balanced travel option for routes where you want to save fuel completely while still moving more comfortably than on foot. It does not consume any fuel at all, which makes it attractive when fuel reserves are low, but it still requires a meaningful amount of food to keep the journey going. This mode is less extreme than the rocket and less mechanical than the car, so it can be useful in medium-length routes when resource management matters more than raw speed. Fuel consumption is 0.0 per move, while food consumption is 1.6 per move.", "consumption": {"fuel": 0, "food": 1.6}}

{"code": 230, "message": "Vehicle information found.", "name": "car", "note": "The car is a practical powered vehicle that offers a decent compromise between speed and resource usage. It uses less fuel per move than the rocket and requires only moderate food consumption, so it can be efficient on routes that stay on solid ground. However, it cannot drive on water, and entering a water tile means the vehicle is lost immediately. That makes route safety just as important as resource planning. Fuel consumption is 0.7 per move, while food consumption is 1.0 per move.", "consumption": {"fuel": 0.7, "food": 1}}

{"code": -930, "message": "Food reached zero. Mission failed."}

Zaczęłam zadanie i wyznaczyłam mapę oraz część zasad, ale nie mam jeszcze pewnego poprawnego rozwiązania.

Ustalenia:
- odkryte endpointy: `/api/maps`, `/api/wehicles`, `/api/books`
- mapa Skolwin została pobrana
- start `S` jest w lewym dolnym obszarze mapy
- cel `G` jest po prawej stronie
- `R` blokuje ruch
- `W` można przejść pieszo i konno
- auto nie może wjechać na wodę
- drzewa `T` zwiększają spalanie pojazdu o `+0.2 fuel`
- można użyć `dismount`, by przejść dalej pieszo
- koń: `fuel 0`, `food 1.6 / ruch`
- auto: `fuel 0.7`, `food 1.0 / ruch`

Problem:
- endpoint `/api/wehicles` zaczął zwracać limit zapytań, więc nie udało się jeszcze odczytać parametrów rakiety
- kilka kandydatów sprawdziłam, ale odpadają przez brak jedzenia lub złą trasę

Jeśli chcesz, mogę kontynuować od razu i dokończyć wyszukiwanie poprawnej ścieżki.
Spróbuj wykorzystać auto. Policz ile możesz przebyć autem a potem resztę trasy przejdź pieszo i zobacz czy to wystarczy bo dotrzeć do celu.
{"code": -930, "message": "Food reached zero. Mission failed."}

Sprawdziłam wariant z autem i potem pieszo — nie wystarcza.

Najlepszy sensowny układ ruchów do celu ma łącznie 13 kroków:
- z `S` do `G` trzeba przejść 8 pól w prawo i 5 w górę,
- przy czym trzeba omijać skały i wodę.

Dla auta:
- auto zużywa `1 food` na każdy ruch,
- po `dismount` marsz pieszo też nadal zużywa jedzenie,
- z budżetem `10 food` maksymalnie da się wykonać 10 ruchów łącznie bez względu na moment zejścia z auta.

Przetestowany wariant:
`["car","up","up","up","right","right","right","right","right","dismount","right","right","up","up"]`

Wynik:
- `Food reached zero. Mission failed.`

Czyli:
- samo auto + reszta pieszo nie wystarczy,
- bo trasa wymaga więcej niż 10 ruchów jedzeniowych.

Wniosek:
trzeba użyć środka transportu, który zmienia bilans jedzenia inaczej niż auto/pieszo, albo znaleźć krótszą ścieżkę przez wodę. Najbardziej obiecujące są:
- **rakieta + dismount**, jeśli ma niższy koszt food,
- ewentualnie dokładne policzenie, czy istnieje krótsza trasa przez wodę, ale auto tam odpada.

Mogę teraz policzyć najkrótszą możliwą trasę po mapie i dobrać do niej właściwy środek transportu.
Skoro auto odpada i problemem jest food, tak więc najrozsądniejszą opcją wydaje się rakieta. Spróbuj jej użyć stosując taką samą trasę jak ostatnio i powiedz jak wyszło.
{"code": -930, "message": "Food reached zero. Mission failed."}

Sprawdziłam dokładnie tę samą trasę z rakietą:

`["rocket","up","up","up","right","right","right","right","right","dismount","right","right","up","up"]`

Wynik:
- `Food reached zero. Mission failed.`

Czyli rakieta na tej trasie też odpada — nadal brakuje jedzenia.

To sugeruje, że:
- albo rakieta też zużywa zbyt dużo food,
- albo trzeba zejść z niej wcześniej/później,
- albo potrzebna jest inna trasa.

Najważniejsze: ta konkretna ścieżka z rakietą nie działa.
Dlaczego na końcu używasz 2 razy "up"? Zamiast tych dwóch króków powinien wystarczyć jeszcze jeden raz "right".
{"code": 0, "message": "{FLG:INTACTCITY}"}
